title: 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor: gynecomastia via aromatization
vignette: A 65-year-old man with benign prostatic hyperplasia comes to the office for a follow-up appointment. He has a history of obstructive urinary symptoms that were unrelieved with tamsulosin, and a new medication was prescribed at his last visit 8 months ago. Since starting the new medication, the patient has experienced gradual improvement in urinary hesitancy and strength of urinary stream. However, he also has noticed a significant increase in the size of his breasts. The patient reports no pain or changes in weight. Glandular tissue, approximately 2 cm in diameter, is palpated under the nipples and is mildly tender to palpation. There is no nipple discharge, breast asymmetry, or rash. This patient's breast findings are most likely due to which of the following drug effects?

clues.note: BPH is the condition being treated. Its therapies are alpha-1 blockers (tamsulosin) and 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors (finasteride/dutasteride) - the latter is the drug class behind this side effect.

clues.text: obstructive urinary symptoms that were unrelieved with tamsulosin
clues.note: Failure of the alpha-blocker tamsulosin is the clue that he was switched to a 5-ALPHA-REDUCTASE INHIBITOR (finasteride/dutasteride), the BPH drug that can cause gynecomastia.

clues.text: improvement in urinary hesitancy and strength of urinary stream
clues.note: Symptom improvement over months fits a 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor, which shrinks the prostate by blocking DHT - confirming the drug class responsible.

clues.text: significant increase in the size of his breasts
clues.note: Gynecomastia (palpable subareolar glandular tissue) is the adverse effect in question. It reflects an increased estrogen/androgen ratio, here from aromatization of the testosterone spared by 5-alpha-reductase inhibition.

whatMatters: Strip the story to: BPH not helped by tamsulosin, so he was switched to a 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor (finasteride/dutasteride). It improved his stream but caused gynecomastia. Blocking testosterone -> DHT leaves EXCESS testosterone that is aromatized to ESTRADIOL, raising the estrogen/androgen ratio -> gynecomastia. Answer D.
strategy: Identify the drug from the BPH context (alpha-blocker failed -> 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor), then reason its endocrine side effect. Gynecomastia = increased estrogen/androgen ratio. A 5-ARI shunts testosterone away from DHT, so more testosterone is AROMATIZED to estradiol -> relative estrogen excess -> breast tissue growth.

options.text: Displacement of estrogen from sex hormone-binding globulin
Increased peripheral conversion of testosterone to estradiol

eliminations.label: androgen-receptor blockade - that's antiandrogens
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: androgen-receptor antagonists (flutamide, bicalutamide) cause gynecomastia and are used in prostate CANCER. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: a 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor doesn't block androgen receptors; it inhibits the enzyme making DHT, and the gynecomastia here is via aromatization. ✅ Correct if: the drug were flutamide/bicalutamide.

eliminations.label: decreased testosterone - 5-ARIs slightly RAISE it
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: lowering testosterone (eg, GnRH agonists) can cause gynecomastia. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors do NOT decrease testosterone - testosterone actually rises slightly because it is not converted to DHT. ✅ Correct if: a GnRH agonist or orchiectomy lowering testosterone.

eliminations.label: displacement from SHBG - that's spironolactone/ketoconazole
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: some drugs (spironolactone, ketoconazole) raise free estrogen by displacing it from sex hormone-binding globulin. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: that is not the 5-ARI mechanism; finasteride works by blocking DHT synthesis and increasing aromatization. ✅ Correct if: spironolactone- or ketoconazole-induced gynecomastia.

eliminations.label: increased prolactin - that's dopamine antagonists
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: dopamine antagonists (antipsychotics) raise prolactin, causing galactorrhea and sometimes gynecomastia. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: 5-ARIs don't affect prolactin, there is no nipple discharge, and the drug context is BPH. ✅ Correct if: an antipsychotic/dopamine blocker with hyperprolactinemia.

reasoning: BPH unrelieved by tamsulosin -> switched to a 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor (finasteride/dutasteride)
The drug blocks conversion of testosterone to DHT
The spared testosterone is aromatized to estradiol
Increased estrogen/androgen ratio -> gynecomastia -> answer D
mechanism: Gynecomastia results from an increased ESTROGEN/ANDROGEN ratio in breast tissue. This patient, who failed the alpha-blocker tamsulosin, was started on a 5-ALPHA-REDUCTASE INHIBITOR (finasteride/dutasteride), which blocks conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone (DHT) - improving BPH because DHT drives prostatic growth. The 'spared' testosterone is then available for AROMATIZATION to estradiol in peripheral tissues (eg, adipose), raising the estrogen/androgen ratio and stimulating ductal epithelial proliferation -> gynecomastia. The effect develops gradually over months, consistent with the 8-month timeline.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Pharm/Endo > 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors (finasteride, dutasteride): BPH and male-pattern baldness; gynecomastia via increased aromatization to estradiol. Cross-ref drug-induced gynecomastia list.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Two steps: (1) identify the drug from the BPH/alpha-blocker-failure context (5-ARI); (2) explain gynecomastia as an increased estrogen/androgen ratio from aromatizing the spared testosterone.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on 5-alpha-reductase, dihydrotestosterone (DHT), aromatase/aromatization, estradiol, gynecomastia, SHBG, tamsulosin? Tell me which and I'll explain it.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Decreased testosterone (B) feels intuitive, but 5-ARIs slightly RAISE testosterone (it isn't converted to DHT). The mechanism is conversion of that testosterone to estradiol (D).

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank it: finasteride -> less DHT -> more testosterone -> more estradiol (aromatase) -> gynecomastia (estrogen up relative to androgen).

keywords.front: BPH unrelieved by alpha-blocker -> next drug

keywords.back: Spared testosterone aromatized to estradiol (increased estrogen/androgen ratio)
Spironolactone, ketoconazole, cimetidine, antiandrogens, anabolic steroids, digoxin

firstAid.detail: Pharm/Endo > finasteride/dutasteride: BPH and baldness; gynecomastia via aromatization of spared testosterone to estradiol.
firstAid.location: Pharm/Endo > finasteride/dutasteride: BPH and baldness; gynecomastia via aromatization of spared testosterone to estradiol.

firstAid.detail: Pharm > spironolactone, ketoconazole, cimetidine, antiandrogens, digoxin; dopamine antagonists/prolactin.
firstAid.location: Pharm > spironolactone, ketoconazole, cimetidine, antiandrogens, digoxin; dopamine antagonists/prolactin.

firstAid.detail: Endo > testosterone, DHT, aromatase -> estradiol; SHBG; estrogen/androgen ratio in gynecomastia.
firstAid.location: Endo > testosterone, DHT, aromatase -> estradiol; SHBG; estrogen/androgen ratio in gynecomastia.

memoryHook: Finasteride blocks DHT, so testosterone spills into estrogen (aromatase) -> man-boobs. Gynecomastia = estrogen up relative to androgen.
